title: Detect stalled agents: running but no token progress
description:
Detect stalled agents using OpenCode session infrastructure instead of token count polling.

CONTEXT: Agents can get stuck in "running" state. Current detection requires manual orch status comparison. But OpenCode already has the signals we need.

BETTER APPROACH (from opencode-fork and agent-lifecycle models):

1. GET /session/status already exists — returns idle/busy/retry per session
2. SSE event stream already flows to daemon — session.status, message.part.updated events
3. Session metadata API (implemented Feb 14) can store beads_id at spawn time

STALL DETECTION:
- Session status = "busy" (model claims to be working)
- No SSE events (message.part.updated, part.updated) for >3 minutes
- This means "busy but silent" = stalled
- Distinct from "idle" (model finished) and "busy with output" (model working)

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. In daemon SSE listener, track last-event-time per session
2. If session is busy and last-event-time > 3 minutes ago, flag as STALLED
3. Surface in dashboard as distinct state (not idle, not busy — stalled)
4. Surface in orch status: "⚠️ STALLED (busy but no output for 3m)"
5. Store beads_id in session metadata at spawn time for direct correlation

DO NOT: Poll token counts via orch status comparison (hacky, unreliable)
USE: OpenCode native signals (session.status + SSE event recency)

REFERENCES:
- .kb/models/opencode-fork.md — GET /session/status, session metadata API, SSE events
- .kb/models/agent-lifecycle-state-model.md — Failure Mode 3: Agent Went Idle But Not Complete
- .kb/models/opencode-session-lifecycle.md — SSE event structure, completion detection

VERIFICATION:
- Agent that stalls (no output for 3m while busy) gets flagged in dashboard
- orch status shows STALLED state
- No false positives on agents doing long tool calls (tool calls still emit SSE events)
- Stall detection uses OpenCode SSE events, not token count polling

title: Cross-repo knowledge inventory — catalog artifacts in ~/.kb/, ~/orch-knowledge/.kb/, ~/Documents/personal/kb-cli/.kb/ that kb reflect misses

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: ORIENTATION_FRAME: Our kb reflect tool only scans the current repo's .kb/ directory. But knowledge artifacts live across multiple locations — ~/.kb/ (global), ~/orch-knowledge/.kb/, ~/Documents/personal/kb-cli/.kb/, and possibly others. We're maintaining a knowledge system with a blind spot over half its territory. Dylan needs to know what's out there so we can decide if kb reflect needs cross-repo awareness or if we handle it differently.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go

SESSION SCOPE: Catalog all knowledge artifacts across repos and global locations that kb reflect currently misses. Produce an inventory with counts, categories, and staleness assessment.

IN SCOPE:
- Inventory ~/.kb/ — list all subdirectories, count artifacts by type (decisions, investigations, models, guides, principles, quick entries)
- Inventory ~/orch-knowledge/.kb/ — same
- Inventory ~/Documents/personal/kb-cli/.kb/ — same if it exists
- Check for .kb/ directories in other repos under ~/Documents/personal/
- For each location: note total artifact count, age range, last-modified dates
- Identify any obvious duplicates or conflicts across repos (e.g., same decision in two places)
- Assess: what percentage of the total knowledge base is invisible to kb reflect?

OUT OF SCOPE:
- Modifying kb reflect code
- Moving or consolidating artifacts
- Deep content review of each artifact

DELIVERABLES:
- SYNTHESIS.md in workspace with complete inventory and gap analysis

VERIFICATION:
- ls and find commands to enumerate artifacts
- wc -l or similar to assess artifact sizes

title: Test: pause trigger 1 (delete after)
Daemon duplicate spawn loop - issue stays triage:ready after first agent spawned
description: Three agents spawned for the same ci-implement-role work because the issue remained triage:ready after the first spawn. The daemon's dedup TTL cache expired between spawns, allowing re-spawning of already-in-progress work. Root cause: daemon should mark issue in_progress immediately on spawn to prevent re-spawn. Currently relies on TTL cache which is fragile. The 3 duplicate agents all detected the duplication themselves and reported it, but the damage (wasted capacity) was already done.

title: Dashboard: split-and-grow node transformation animation
description:
When an agent completes and produces a knowledge artifact, animate the transformation from issue node to artifact node using split-and-grow.

DESIGN DOC: docs/designs/2026-02-15-collaborative-tree-building.md (Phase 3)

SCOPE:
1. Detect when an issue completion produces a .kb/ artifact (investigation, decision, model)
2. Animate: issue ● node fades/shrinks to small gray node
3. Animate: new ◉/★/◆ artifact node grows from it with branch line connecting them
4. Smooth CSS transitions for state changes (triage → in-progress → complete)
5. Pulsing stops and node solidifies on completion, then triggers split-and-grow

DEPENDS ON: orch-go-krgg (dashboard tree component)

VERIFICATION:
- When agent completes and produces investigation, split-and-grow animation plays
- Old issue node remains visible (small, gray) with connecting edge to new artifact
- Animation is smooth, not jarring
- Works for all artifact types (investigation, decision, model)

Redesign coaching metrics + integrate into orch stats + auto-surface at session start
description:
Three-part feature:

## 1. Metric Redesign

ORCHESTRATOR - Strip noise, keep signal:
- KEEP: frame_collapse (delegation rule violation), circular_pattern (contradicting prior knowledge)
- KEEP but tune: behavioral_variation (useful for spawn retry loops)
- KILL: action_ratio (378 fires, wrong signal for orchestrators), analysis_paralysis (352 fires, consecutive bash is normal), compensation_pattern (79 fires, too loose at 30% overlap), context_ratio (unclear value), dylan_signal_prefix (never fired), premise_skipping (never fired)
- ADD: spawn_without_context (no kb context in recent tool window before orch spawn), completion_backlog (agents at Phase:Complete >10 min without orch complete), direct_implementation (Edit/Write on files outside .kb/ .orch/ CLAUDE.md AGENTS.md)

Result: 5 orchestrator metrics (2 kept, 3 new) down from 8 noisy ones.

WORKER - Keep all 4 as-is, let them accumulate real data:
- tool_failure_rate (3+ consecutive failures)
- context_usage (estimated tokens, alert at 80%)
- time_in_phase (minutes since phase change, alert at 15+)
- commit_gap (time since last commit, alert at 30+ min)

## 2. Stats Integration

Add coaching summary section to orch stats output:
```
🧠 BEHAVIORAL HEALTH (coaching plugin, last N days)
  Orchestrator:
    Frame collapse:       3 events (2 sessions)
    Circular patterns:    1 event
    Completion backlog:   2 alerts
    Direct implementation: 0 events
  Workers:
    Context exhaustion:   2 alerts (>80%)
    Tool failure runs:    1 alert
    Stuck in phase:       0 alerts
    Commit gaps:          4 alerts
```

Read from ~/.orch/coaching-metrics.jsonl, aggregate by metric_type for the stats time window.

## 3. Session Start Auto-Surface

Run orch stats --days 1 summary automatically when orchestrator session starts. Could be:
- Plugin hook on first tool call in new session
- session.prompt.before hook if available
- Injection via coaching plugin detecting new orchestrator session

Goal: orchestrator sees system health picture without remembering to run the command.

## Evidence
- coaching-metrics.jsonl has 1000+ entries, dominated by action_ratio (378) and analysis_paralysis (352) noise
- Worker metrics just started working (Feb 14 fix) - context_usage verified
- orch stats already reads events.jsonl, adding coaching-metrics.jsonl pass is natural
- Current session start is skill doc guideline only, not automated

## Key files
- plugins/coaching.ts (metric detection, 1831 lines)
- cmd/orch/stats.go (stats aggregation)
- .opencode/plugin/coaching.ts (deployed copy, must stay in sync)

title: Improve daemon skill inference with description-aware heuristics and accuracy tracking
description: Two gaps in daemon skill inference (pkg/daemon/skill_inference.go): (1) DESCRIPTION-AWARE HEURISTICS: Current inference only uses issue type + skill:* labels + title prefix. It never reads the description. Add keyword/pattern matching on description text to improve inference. Signal words: 'audit/analyze/correlate/investigate/understand/how does' → investigation, 'fix/broken/error/crash/fails' → systematic-debugging (when cause is described) or architect (when vague), 'compare/evaluate/research/best practice' → research. This should be a fallback AFTER label and title checks but BEFORE type-based inference. Keep it simple — keyword matching, not NLP. (2) INFERENCE ACCURACY TRACKING: Add an event type 'spawn.skill_inferred' to events.jsonl that logs {issue_id, inferred_skill, issue_type, title, had_skill_label, had_title_match, used_description_heuristic}. This enables post-hoc analysis of inference quality. Also fix: InferSkillFromTitle still references kb-reflect which is being removed (orch-go-bt7). Key files: pkg/daemon/skill_inference.go (114 lines), pkg/daemon/skill_inference_test.go, pkg/daemon/daemon.go (where inference is called). Verify: go test ./pkg/daemon/ passes, go build ./... passes. PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go

title: Deploy orchestrator skill with verifiability-first completion review protocol
description:
The orchestrator skill source (.skillc/SKILL.md, checksum 252b37c8c60a) contains the two-gate completion review protocol (explain-back, anti-sycophancy, behavioral gates). But neither deployment target has it:
- ~/.claude/skills/ (checksum 61797c7c8a98, last modified Feb 14 18:43)  
- ~/.opencode/skill/ (checksum 779efe80fbde, last modified Feb 13 14:29)

Agent orch-go-6th updated the source on Feb 14 23:24 but never ran skillc deploy. The orchestrator that loaded the stale skill had zero instructions about verifiability-first, so it fell back to the old completion flow (orchestrator as judge, batch-completing light-tier) under velocity pressure.

FIX: Run skillc deploy to push source SKILL.md to both deployment targets. Verify checksums match after deploy.

EVIDENCE: grep for 'explain-back' in source returns 11 matches, in deployed returns 0.

title: Enrich spawn context with orch tree --cluster for area awareness
description:
Before spawning into an area, the orchestrator should see the full knowledge landscape for that area. This improves skill selection, prompt quality, and prevents duplicate work.

CONTEXT: Currently kb context provides keyword search. orch tree --cluster provides structural awareness — what investigations exist, what decisions were made, what is untested. Together they give the orchestrator full area context before spawning.

SCOPE:
1. When orchestrator runs orch spawn, auto-detect the area from issue labels or task description
2. If area maps to a known cluster, inject orch tree --cluster <area> --depth 3 output into the spawn prompt or show it to orchestrator before spawn confirmation
3. Add orch tree --cluster <area> --format summary flag that produces a 3-5 line area briefing:
   - N investigations, M decisions, K models, J open issues
   - Health smells if any
   - Most recent artifact date
4. Consider adding this to SPAWN_CONTEXT.md template so agents also see where their work fits

DEPENDS ON: health smells and relationship parsing for full value, but basic cluster summary works with current tree.

VERIFICATION:
- orch tree --cluster X --format summary produces concise area briefing
- Spawning into an area with a known cluster surfaces the cluster context
- Agents see where their work fits in the knowledge landscape
- go build ./... succeeds
- go test ./... passes

title: [orch-go] systematic-debugging: Build is broken: cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go:536:1 ha...
Reorganize orchestrator skill around 'keep Dylan oriented' as primary frame
description:
The orchestrator skill (~1,251 lines) is currently organized around what the orchestrator does (delegate, triage, synthesize). But Dylan's experience of working with an orchestrator should feel like being narrated through a journey, not presented with checkboxes. The completion review section describes a mechanical two-gate process, but Dylan reports the ceremony feels disconnected — he lacks the frame to understand why completed work matters by the time it arrives.

Core insight from conversation: the comprehension problem isn't at completion time, it's at spawn time. The 'why Dylan cares' frame decays between spawn and completion, and nothing preserves it. The explain-back gate assumes Dylan has context he's already lost.

Proposed reframe: organize the entire skill around 'is Dylan oriented?' at each moment:
- At spawn: establish the frame (what are we doing and why)
- During work: maintain narrative continuity (things are progressing)  
- At completion: reconnect to original motivation, resolve, place contextually (1 of 3 blockers for X)
- At session boundaries: 'previously on...' orientation

The mechanical constraints (delegation rule, verification gates, daemon management) don't disappear but become implementation detail in service of orientation, not the organizing frame.

Key constraint: the skill is auto-generated from templates at ~/orch-knowledge/skills/src/meta/orchestrator/.skillc/ — architect must propose structure changes to template sources, not SKILL.md directly.

Risk: current skill is battle-tested, every rule from a real failure. New frame must be at least as protective while being more coherent.

title: Implement orchestrator skill orientation redesign - template rewrite
description:
Implement the accepted design from orch-go-1003 (architect investigation at .kb/investigations/2026-02-16-design-orchestrator-skill-orientation-redesign.md).

Phase 1: Rewrite the orchestrator skill template at ~/orch-knowledge/skills/src/meta/orchestrator/.skillc/

Key changes:
1. Reorganize top-level sections from action-based (COMPREHEND/TRIAGE/SYNTHESIZE) to four-moment structure (At Spawn Time / During Work / At Completion / At Session Boundaries)
2. Update identity from 'Strategic Comprehender' to 'Strategic Comprehender who keeps Dylan oriented' with ORIENT → DELEGATE → RECONNECT operating model
3. Replace completion review explain-back with three-layer reconnection model (frame reconnection, resolution, contextual placement) within Gate 1
4. Add frame capture checklist to spawn-time section
5. Consolidate hard constraints into dedicated section
6. Move session start/end protocols from buried Meta-Orchestrator Interface to top-level session boundaries section

Constraint mapping table in design investigation shows all load-bearing patterns survive.
~55% content stays in place, ~30% moves, ~10% new, ~5% removed (redundant).

Design investigation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-16-design-orchestrator-skill-orientation-redesign.md
Decision on identity: 'Strategic Comprehender who keeps Dylan oriented' (hybrid)
Decision on frame friction: medium (required field, defaults to task description)

The frame captures 'why Dylan cares' in his terms at spawn time, travels through the workspace/beads, and gets read back at completion time for the three-layer reconnection model.

Also add bd comment with FRAME: annotation at spawn time so orchestrator can read it back during orch complete.

title: Disable all inherited GitHub Actions workflows in opencode fork
description: 31 workflows inherited from upstream (anomalyco/opencode) are running on our fork (dylan-conlin/opencode) and generating distracting alerts. These include CI, publishing, Discord notifications, stale issue management, PR management, deploy, etc. — none of which are needed on our fork. Disable all of them. The simplest approach is likely adding a top-level condition to each workflow file or removing/renaming them. The fork is at ~/Documents/personal/opencode-fork with origin at git@github.com:dylan-conlin/opencode.git.

title: Fix daemon test failures introduced by orch-go-nff fail-fast change
description: orch-go-nff changed expandTriageReadyEpics to return error (fail-fast), but 5 daemon tests now fail: TestDaemon_Once_ProcessesOneIssue, TestDaemon_Run_ProcessesAllIssues, TestDaemon_Run_RespectsMaxIterations, TestDaemon_Once_WithPool_AcquiresSlot, TestDaemon_OnceWithSlot_ReturnsSlot (panic). Root causes: (1) callers in tests not updated for new 3-return signature, (2) indentation bug in expandTriageReadyEpics (return statements lost tabs). Fix: update test mocks/callers for 3-return expandTriageReadyEpics, fix indentation.

title: bd sync hangs — second occurrence in 24 hours
description: bd sync is hanging repeatedly. First occurrence: earlier today during agent completion flow. Second occurrence: just now, orch status also timed out (likely calls beads internally). Earlier we saw bd sync fail with 'cannot pull with rebase: You have unstaged changes' — but that should be an error, not a hang. Two possibilities: (1) git lock contention from multiple agents committing simultaneously, (2) actual deadlock in bd sync logic. The beads repo had a recent fix for bd sync deadlock (commit in beads repo). Need to verify we're running the patched version and investigate whether the hang is git-level or beads-level.

title: Model strategy: evaluate Claude model lineup for orchestration workflow
description: Sonnet 4.6 launched (Feb 17 2026) — approaches Opus-level intelligence at Sonnet pricing. Users preferred it to Opus 4.5 59% of the time. Less overengineering, better instruction following, 1M context window. Anthropic says 'Opus 4.6 remains the strongest for deepest reasoning.' We currently default to sonnet for headless spawns and use --opus for complex skills (debugging, investigation, architect, audit, research). Questions to answer: (1) Should Sonnet 4.6 replace Opus for any currently-Opus skills? (2) Is Opus 4.6 still worth the Max subscription cost for agent work? (3) What's the right model for each skill type given current pricing and capability? (4) Should we add Sonnet 4.6 as an explicit model option in orch spawn? (5) How does the 1M context window change our context management strategy? Reference: https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-sonnet-4-6 Current model selection: skill/meta/orchestrator SKILL.md Model Selection section.

title: Fix dashboard blind to tmux agents: reorder scans in serve_agents.go
description:
Three compounding bugs make tmux agents invisible in dashboard:

1. Scan ordering (Critical): serve_agents.go workspace scan (line 598) runs before tmux scan (line 551). Workspace scan claims active tmux agents as 'completed'. Fix: move tmux scan before workspace scan.

2. Beads enrichment skip (Critical): serve_agents.go lines 700-703 skip beads phase comments for workspace-scanned entries, so phase is always null. Fix: include beads enrichment for entries that have a beads_id.

3. active_agent not populated (Important): serve_beads.go GraphNode struct lacks active_agent field. Frontend expects it. Fix: join agent data by beads_id in handleBeadsGraph().

4. WIP store stub (Nice-to-have): wip.ts setRunningAgents() is a no-op, making runningAgentDetailsByIssueId always empty for ALL agents.

Root cause documented in: .kb/models/agent-lifecycle-state-model/probes/2026-02-17-dashboard-blind-to-tmux-agents.md
repro: Spawn a Claude CLI tmux agent, check dashboard — shows 'No active agent linked' and status=completed despite agent running in tmux

title: Dashboard work-graph usability improvements: visibility, sorting, and clarity
description:
The work-graph Issues view is structurally working (dependency trees, Ready to Complete, independent issues) but has several usability gaps that make it hard to orient at a glance:

**1. In-progress issues don't visually stand out**
The play icon + green subline is too subtle. P1 in-progress bugs should pop — left border accent, background highlight, or similar treatment to the Ready to Complete section.

**2. P1s buried in INDEPENDENT ISSUES**
Two P1 bugs are mixed in with 25 P2s. Priority should sort within sections — P1s at top, not mixed by creation order.

**3. 'No active agent linked' is noisy and negative**
Shown on every in-progress issue (partly due to orch-go-1022 tmux bug). Even when fixed, the subline should show agent phase + runtime when linked, and just 'unassigned' or nothing when not — not an alarming message.

**4. Ready to Complete shows stale/already-closed entries**
980.1 (already closed) and 990 (failed Codex attempt with stale Phase: Complete) still appear. Section should only show issues that are actually open + Phase: Complete.

**5. 27 independent issues is overwhelming**
Most are backlog. Default should show active work (in-progress + recently created) and collapse/paginate the rest. Or separate 'Active' vs 'Backlog' sections.

**6. Completed agents section competes with Ready to Complete**
The completed agents banner (token counts, timing) is useful but visually competes with the more actionable Ready to Complete section. Consider collapsing by default or moving to Completed tab.

**7. Dependency chain header uses issue title as section name**
Chain header shows the title of issue 980.4 which reads oddly as a group name. Should use the root issue title, a computed chain name, or just the root issue ID.

title: Design and implement orch rework command for sending agents back with specific completion review feedback
description: Completion review regularly finds gaps in agent work (e.g. xu8l shipped without removing flag definitions, sg4k sort has no effect). Currently the workaround is spawning a fresh agent with REWORK prefix in the prompt. A dedicated orch rework <beads-id> 'feedback' command would: (1) reopen the issue, (2) inject the rework feedback as context alongside the original SYNTHESIS.md, (3) spawn a new agent against the same issue with both the original task and the rework feedback, (4) track it as a rework (not a fresh attempt) for completion metrics. This is a natural extension of the completion workflow.

title: DetermineSpawnBackend ignores user config (~/.orch/config.yaml) backend setting — only checks project config
description: DetermineSpawnBackend (extraction.go:612) has a config resolution bug. When --model is explicit but --backend is not, it checks projCfg.SpawnMode (project config) but never checks userconfig.Load().Backend (user config at ~/.orch/config.yaml). The user has backend: opencode in their global config, but projects without their own .orch/config.yaml fall through to the hardcoded default of claude. Fix: add user config fallback in the resolution chain: explicit --backend > project config > user config > hardcoded default.
repro: Set backend: opencode in ~/.orch/config.yaml. Spawn with --model codex in a project without .orch/config.yaml SpawnMode. Agent spawns on claude backend instead of respecting user config default. User config has backend: opencode but DetermineSpawnBackend line 641 only checks projCfg.SpawnMode, never userconfig.

title: orch work / daemon spawn ignores default_model from user config — always spawns on sonnet/claude
description: runWork() in spawn_cmd.go:284 calls runSpawnWithSkillInternal without setting spawnModel. The user config has default_model: codex but orch work never reads it. This means every daemon-spawned agent ignores the user's default model preference, burning Max subscription tokens on claude when they should be running on codex via opencode. Fix: runWork should load userconfig, read default_model, and set spawnModel before calling runSpawnWithSkillInternal. This is the daemon path — it affects ALL auto-spawned work.
repro: Set default_model: codex in ~/.orch/config.yaml. Let daemon auto-spawn via orch work. Agent spawns on claude/sonnet instead of codex/opencode. runWork() in spawn_cmd.go never reads userconfig default_model.

title: Test issue for verifying daemon model selection
OpenAI/Codex auth is project-scoped in OpenCode — headless agents fail intermittently, price-watch can't use codex at all
description: Two related symptoms: (1) Three headless codex agents spawned at 10:12 AM dead (0 tokens), but one spawned at 10:54 works fine — suggesting transient or project-context-dependent initialization. (2) Price-watch project can't spawn codex agents despite valid auth.json — PW has a .env file that orch-go doesn't, and OpenCode may resolve auth differently per project. Root hypothesis: Codex plugin auth initialization is project-scoped, and something about per-project context (env files, config, provider state) affects whether OpenAI OAuth tokens are loaded.
repro: 1. orch spawn with openai/gpt-5.2-codex model via opencode backend (headless). 2. Session is created (200 OK from /session). 3. Prompt sent (204 from /prompt_async). 4. Session shows 0 tokens, idle status. 5. Three agents (3e5, fq5, 1026) all exhibit this. Evidence: Codex OAuth plugin may not initialize providers in headless mode, or the Codex Responses API endpoint differs from standard OpenAI Chat API that headless mode uses.

If Provider.state async initializer rejects (e.g., auth.json being written, models.dev timeout), rejected Promise cached per-directory. All subsequent requests fail until Instance eviction (30min) or server restart.

title: Add /api prefix mount to OpenCode server for external tool compatibility
description: The OpenCode server SPA catch-all is swallowing all /api/* routes, returning frontend HTML instead of JSON responses. This is the root cause of a cascade of observability failures: orch status shows phantom agents with wrong model/skill/runtime, orch review can't detect completions, dashboard Swarm Map shows 0 active agents, SSE stream receives no session events, work-graph shows unassigned instead of agent info. Spawn pipeline itself works (sessions are created and execute successfully), but nothing can observe them afterward. Discovered during price-watch codex spawn testing on 2026-02-18.
repro: curl -s http://127.0.0.1:4096/api/sessions | head -3 returns HTML doctype instead of JSON. Same for /api/session/<id>, /api/session/<id>/messages. Tested with both orch-go and price-watch x-opencode-directory headers — both return HTML.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Planning - Investigating OpenCode server routing order for /api endpoints

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Reproduction verified: curl -s http://127.0.0.1:4096/api/sessions returned HTML doctype pre-fix; after rebuild, ran opencode serve on 4097 and curl -s http://127.0.0.1:4097/api/sessions returned JSON session list; curl -s http://127.0.0.1:4097/api/session/ses_38d727ffaffeE9R8sABe5d2Den/message returned JSON.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Complete - Mounted API router under /api and added /sessions alias to avoid SPA proxy swallowing /api/*; verified /api/sessions and /api/session/:id/message return JSON. Tests: bun run build (OK).

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: CORRECTION: The /api/* prefix never existed in OpenCode and orch-go's client never used it. orch-go calls /session, /session/status, etc. (no /api prefix) which always worked. The HTML response was only observed when the price-watch orchestrator manually curled /api/sessions — a URL that never existed. The agent's fix correctly added /api as a new mount point (useful for external tools/curl), but this was NOT the root cause of the phantom agents or observability issues. The improvement in orch status after the fix was likely due to the server restart clearing cached state, not the route change. Downgrading from P0 — the observability issues (phantoms, stale metrics) have a different root cause that needs separate investigation.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
EXPLAIN-BACK:
Added /api prefix mount to OpenCode server. Useful for external tools but not the root cause of phantom issues as we originally thought.

title: Tmux spawn times out — timeout waiting for OpenCode TUI to be ready after 15s
description: The --tmux flag triggers a TUI readiness check with a 15s timeout that fails. Headless spawns work fine. Discovered during price-watch codex spawn testing. The tmux spawn path uses opencode attach mode which requires the TUI to initialize — the readiness probe may be checking the wrong signal or the TUI startup is slower than 15s for cross-project directories.
repro: From price-watch directory: orch spawn --bypass-triage hello 'Say hello and exit' --tmux — fails with 'timeout waiting for OpenCode TUI to be ready after 15s'. Headless spawn of same task succeeds immediately.

title: 96 phantom agents and stale session metrics — root cause unknown
description: orch status shows 96 phantom agents and session metrics from a 689h-old session. Originally attributed to OpenCode API routing (orch-go-1033) but that was disproven — orch-go's client uses /session not /api/session and those endpoints work fine. Server restart reduced phantoms from showing but didn't eliminate them. Possible causes: 1) Sessions accumulate in OpenCode's session store and are never cleaned up. 2) orch's registry tracks sessions that no longer exist on the server. 3) The /session/status endpoint returns {} (empty) which may cause orch to classify all sessions as phantom. 4) 689h session metrics suggest the session tracking state file is never reset. Need to understand the full lifecycle: how does a session go from spawned → active → complete → cleaned up, and where is that chain breaking?

title: tmux spawn fails inside overmind due to socket mismatch
description: When running orch spawn inside overmind tmux, SessionExists targets main socket while CreateWindow/SendKeys target current socket, so workers-* session appears to exist but window creation fails in overmind.
repro: Run: orch spawn --bypass-triage --tmux investigation "tmux readiness probe check" from overmind tmux; observe error: failed to create tmux window: exit status 1.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: tmux spawn fails inside overmind because SessionExists checks the main tmux socket while CreateWindow/SendKeys target the current (overmind) socket. When inside an overmind tmux session, the workers-orch-go session appears to exist on the main socket but window creation fails because it's actually on overmind's socket.

Reproduce: run 'orch spawn --bypass-triage --tmux investigation "test"' from inside an overmind tmux session. Observe 'failed to create tmux window: exit status 1'.

Fix: make tmux operations consistently use the same socket. Either detect overmind and use its socket, or always target the main tmux server.

Key files: pkg/tmux/tmux.go

VERIFICATION: go build ./cmd/orch/ && go vet ./cmd/orch/ && go test ./pkg/tmux/...

Converted all 21 bare calls across pkg/tmux/tmux.go, pkg/orch/extraction.go, and pkg/spawn/backends/tmux.go to use tmuxCommand()/tmux.SelectWindow(). Tests: go test ./pkg/tmux/... - 31 passed, 0 failed (0.5s). No discovered work.

Fixed tmux socket inconsistency: all 21 bare exec.Command(tmux) calls converted to tmuxCommand() for consistent socket targeting. The Jan 15 fix only wired SessionExists — now all tmux operations use the same socket. 31 tests pass, build clean.

title: Fix orch status phantom logic — align with state.LivenessResult.IsPhantom
description: orch status in cmd/orch/status_cmd.go has its own phantom classification that marks agents as phantom without checking beads open/closed status. title: Investigation: skillc deploy pipeline is a source of chronic rework and confusion — audit source/compiled/deployed file relationships and fix
description: Repeated pattern: skill fixes get applied to the wrong file, deployed to the wrong location, or deployed correctly but not picked up by running sessions. This has caused at least 3 rounds of rework on the orchestrator skill alone (orch-go-1036, orch-go-1037, orch-go-1044). The problems: (1) Multiple copies of the same skill exist across different paths — source template, compiled intermediate, deployed copy, and sometimes stale copies in other directories. Nobody knows which file is authoritative. (2) Workers edit deployed artifacts instead of source templates because the relationship is unclear. skillc deploy then overwrites their work. (3) skillc deploy --target deploys to a root SKILL.md, not into the meta/orchestrator/ subdirectory where the old manual copy lives, creating two competing files. (4) Running sessions cache the skill at load time — deploying a fix has no effect until session restart, but there is no signal that this is the case. (5) orch-go-1015 (P3, open) already identified 5 stale orchestrator skill copies. The investigation should map the full lifecycle: source template → skillc compile → skillc deploy → session load → runtime. Identify every point of confusion and propose a simplified pipeline.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Dylan has been told twice that the orchestrator skill was fixed. Both times it wasn't actually fixed where it mattered. The skillc pipeline has too many copies, unclear authority, and no feedback when a deploy doesn't reach running sessions. This causes chronic rework and erodes trust in the fix/verify/close cycle.

title: Headless codex spawns silently die — session created but not visible in OpenCode, no error surfaced to user
description: This is THE blocker for codex adoption. Config is now respected (orch-go-1045), orchestrator skill knows codex (orch-go-1044), but spawns silently die. State.create rejected Promise caching was fixed (orch-go-1031) but sessions still vanish. Need to trace what happens between orch spawn returning a session ID and the session actually running. Possible causes: auth failure for codex model, project-scoped OpenCode auth missing for price-watch, session init timeout, or the session dying immediately after creation with no error propagation.
repro: From price-watch orchestrator: orch spawn --bypass-triage --backend opencode --model codex feature-impl for pw-8953. Spawn command returns success with session ID ses_38d10600bffeNNAQs1krypfg5E. Session does not appear in curl http://127.0.0.1:4096/sessions. Dashboard cannot see the agent. No error shown to user. Agent is gone.

title: Headless spawn must verify session exists and surface prompt_async errors — fire-and-forget creates silent failures
description: Two fixes needed: (1) After CreateSession + SendMessageInDirectory, verify session exists by GET /session/:id with correct x-opencode-directory header. If session missing or errored, surface error to user instead of reporting success. (2) Dashboard session listing should pass x-opencode-directory or query all projects. See investigation: orch-go-1051 SYNTHESIS.md for full trace.
repro: orch spawn --bypass-triage --backend opencode --model codex feature-impl for any task. Spawn returns success with session ID. If model/auth fails, prompt_async logs error server-side only. orch-go reports success. Session invisible from dashboard because x-opencode-directory not passed.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Fix headless spawn to verify session creation and surface prompt_async errors instead of silently reporting success.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: Headless codex spawns report success but the agent is silently dead. orch-go fires CreateSession + prompt_async and walks away. If auth/model fails, the error is only logged server-side. User sees 'Spawned agent (headless)' when the agent is DOA.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Small

## Root Cause (from orch-go-1051)
1. prompt_async returns 204 immediately. Errors only emitted as session.error SSE events.
2. Session listing is project-scoped by x-opencode-directory header.
3. orch-go never verifies the session after creating it.

## The Fix
In pkg/opencode/client.go: After CreateSession + SendMessageInDirectory, verify session exists with GET /session/:id and correct directory header. Brief SSE subscribe (2-3s) to catch session.error. Return error if session dead.

In pkg/orch/extraction.go (headless spawn path): Check verification result. Print error instead of success if session is dead.

## Scope
IN: Post-spawn verification in orch-go. Surface errors.
OUT: No OpenCode server changes.

## Verification
go test ./pkg/opencode/ ./pkg/orch/

title: [orch-go] systematic-debugging: Fix GPT/codex agents not committing work or rep...

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Fix GPT/codex agents not committing work or reporting phase.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: Dylan spent an entire day where 16 of 20 price-watch agents did their work but never committed or reported phase. The code was on disk but never git committed. We just spawned an opus agent to fix this and IT DID THE SAME THING — wrote SYNTHESIS claiming success but made zero file changes. The problem is self-demonstrating.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Small

## Root Cause (already identified by prior agent)

The SPAWN_CONTEXT.md template in pkg/spawn/context.go says 'After your final commit' but NEVER includes explicit git add and git commit commands. Anthropic models infer this. GPT models do not.

## The Fix (simple and specific)

In pkg/spawn/context.go, find the SESSION COMPLETE PROTOCOL sections and add explicit git commit instructions with code blocks. There are 3 sections that need updating:

1. Main tracked spawn completion (~lines 162-204) — for both light and full tier
2. NoTrack/ad-hoc spawn completion (~lines 136-160)  
3. Final step reminder at end of template (~lines 399-428)

Add to each section, BEFORE the bd comment and /exit steps:
```
git add -A && git commit -m 'feat/fix: <description of changes>'
```

Also update pkg/spawn/context_test.go to expect the new commit instructions.

## Scope
IN: Edit pkg/spawn/context.go and pkg/spawn/context_test.go. Run tests. COMMIT YOUR CHANGES.
OUT: Nothing else.

## CRITICAL: YOU MUST COMMIT
After making changes:
1. git add -A
2. git commit -m 'fix: add explicit git commit instructions to spawn context for GPT model compliance'
3. go test ./pkg/spawn/ ./pkg/orch/
4. bd comment add orch-go-49025 'Phase: Complete - ...' (this may fail if issue doesn't exist, that's OK)

## Verification
go test ./pkg/spawn/ ./pkg/orch/

title: Rethink agent observability architecture — current multi-source discovery is unfixable

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Strategic redesign: agent observability architecture.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: Dylan spent all of Feb 18 firefighting observability bugs — price-watch agents reported as dead when they were running fine, 84/85 agents showing empty metadata in dashboard, cross-repo visibility broken despite 6 weeks of fixes. The incremental fix approach has failed. We need a clean design.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Large

## Context

The current agent observability layer has 5 interacting data sources:
1. Workspace scanning (.orch/workspace/ directories with SPAWN_CONTEXT.md)
2. Tmux window discovery (scanning for emoji-prefixed window names)  
3. OpenCode session API (HTTP queries to localhost:4096)
4. Beads enrichment (reading phase comments from beads issues)
5. Agent registry / state.db (legacy, stale, not written to)

These are reconciled in serve_agents.go and serve_agents_cache.go. The reconciliation is where every bug lives.

## Key Constraints Discovered

- OpenCode --attach mode sets session directory from server's cwd, not CLI's cwd (cross-project agents all show orch-go as directory)
- kb projects integration adds price-watch to workspace scanning, but enrichment fails silently
- Workspace scan and tmux scan can claim the same agent with different metadata
- Beads enrichment is skipped for workspace-scanned entries (performance optimization that breaks correctness)
- Agent registry hasn't been written to today; state.db is 6 days stale — both are effectively dead

## Strategic Questions to Answer

1. Should there be ONE authoritative source of truth for agent state? If so, which one?
2. What's the minimal data model the dashboard needs? (id, status, phase, project, model — what else?)
3. Can we eliminate the multi-source reconciliation entirely?
4. How should cross-repo agents work? (spawned from orch-go, running in price-watch)
5. What happens when the source of truth is unavailable? (graceful degradation vs hard failure)

## Artifacts to Read

- cmd/orch/serve_agents.go — current multi-source agent discovery
- cmd/orch/serve_agents_cache.go — workspace cache with kb projects integration
- cmd/orch/serve_beads.go — beads graph API with active_agent
- pkg/opencode/client.go — OpenCode session queries
- .orch/workspace/archived/og-inv-dashboard-blind-claude-17feb-24d6-213340/SYNTHESIS.md — root cause of scan ordering bug
- .orch/workspace/archived/og-debug-fix-dashboard-blind-17feb-1b59/SYNTHESIS.md — fix that didn't stick
- .orch/workspace/archived/og-arch-cross-project-agents-07jan-1844/SYNTHESIS.md — cross-project design
- .orch/workspace/archived/og-debug-investigate-headless-codex-18feb-0eb3/SYNTHESIS.md — headless visibility

## Deliverable

An investigation with a clear recommendation for a simplified agent observability architecture. Should include:
- Proposed single source of truth (or argued case for why multi-source is necessary)
- Data model for agent state
- How cross-repo agents are tracked
- Migration path from current to proposed
- What gets deleted vs kept

Write to: .kb/investigations/2026-02-18-design-agent-observability-rethink.md

title: Implement work-centric agent discovery — invert from infrastructure-first to beads-first
description:
Core observability rewrite. Current serve_agents.go discovers agents from infrastructure (OpenCode sessions, tmux windows) then reconciles 5 data sources to figure out what work they represent. This is backwards and the reconciliation is where every bug lives (~1400 lines).

New model: Start from beads issues (status=in_progress), find workspace by beads_id, get session_id from workspace, check OpenCode for liveness only. O(n) single-pass instead of O(n*m) reconciliation.

Combined approach from two architects:
- Architect A (Feb 14): Move state into OpenCode sessions — infrastructure half-built (session metadata API, AGENT_MANIFEST.json)
- Architect B (Feb 18): Invert discovery — start from beads (work), not sessions (infrastructure)

Key artifacts:
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-18-design-agent-observability-rethink.md
- .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-lifecycle-ownership-own-accept-build.md
- .orch/workspace/og-arch-strategic-redesign-agent-18feb-0d2d/SYNTHESIS.md

Depends on: orch-go-1059 (delete dead registry first), orch-go-1060 (cross-project fix informs rewrite)

title: Wire OpenCode session metadata into agent discovery — complete the half-built bridge from Feb 14 decision
description:
The Feb 14 decision (.kb/decisions/2026-02-14-lifecycle-ownership-own-accept-build.md) designed a 3-step plan to move agent state into OpenCode sessions. The OpenCode fork already has the features built:
- Session metadata API (commit 36f084ca5) — store beads_id, workspace, tier
- Session TTL (commit f3c3865b8) — configurable expiry
- Worker detection (commits 459a1bfba, 0922edfe7)
- State.create cache fix (commit b6823f82b)

And orch-go spawn already calls CreateSession() with metadata and writes AGENT_MANIFEST.json.

But serve_agents.go never consumed any of this. After 1061 lands (beads-first discovery), the next step is wiring the OpenCode session metadata as the liveness/identity lookup instead of the current SSE polling and session list scanning.

Steps from the decision:
1. Wire GET /session/status endpoint (eliminates ~1,400 lines of SSE status parsing)
2. Read session metadata for beads_id/workspace/tier (eliminates ~800 lines of workspace cross-reference)
3. Use session TTL for cleanup (eliminates ~1,200 lines of cleanup code)

Key artifacts:
- .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-lifecycle-ownership-own-accept-build.md
- .kb/models/opencode-fork.md
- pkg/opencode/client.go — already has CreateSession with metadata

title: Add integration tests for beads-first agent discovery in serve_agents.go
description:
orch-go-1061 rewrote agent discovery to start from beads issues instead of scanning OpenCode sessions/tmux. Zero tests were written. This is the same pattern that caused the Feb 18 nightmare — code ships without tests, breaks weeks later, fix causes another break.

Test coverage needed for the new discovery path:

1. discoverAgentsFromWork / listInProgressIssues:
   - Single-project: returns in_progress issues, ignores open/closed
   - Cross-project: queries multiple project dirs, deduplicates
   - Empty project dirs: falls back to default ListOpenIssues
   - Error handling: one project fails, others still returned

2. handleAgents beads-first loop:
   - Workspace found: session_id, spawn_time, project_dir populated from workspace
   - Workspace not found: agent still returned with beads data only
   - Session found: status derived from activity (active/idle/dead thresholds)
   - Session not found: agent shows as dead
   - Cross-project: correct project_dir from workspace, not session.directory

3. Cache behavior:
   - projectDirsMatchForIDs: cache invalidates when project context changes
   - Beads cache keyed by project dir (from orch-go-1060)

4. Edge cases:
   - --no-track agents (no beads issue)
   - Completed agents with SYNTHESIS.md
   - Stale agent filtering (displayThreshold)
   - Agent with beads issue but no workspace yet (just spawned)

Key files:
- cmd/orch/serve_agents.go — the code to test
- cmd/orch/serve_agents_test.go — existing tests to extend
- cmd/orch/serve_agents_cache.go — cache logic
- cmd/orch/serve_agents_cache_test.go — existing cache tests

title: Architect: Design unified spawn config precedence model — 10 bugs from competing config sources

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Architect: Design unified spawn config precedence model — 10 bugs from competing config sources

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Planning - Reviewing spawn config sources and precedence

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Every few days a new bug surfaces where user intent (model, backend, mode) gets lost or overridden somewhere in the spawn pipeline. We've fixed 10 of these individually. The root cause is there's no single config resolution function — user config, project config, explicit flags, infrastructure escape hatch, and daemon defaults all compete with no defined precedence.

ARCHITECT SCOPE:
- Audit all config sources that affect spawn: explicit CLI flags, user config (~/.orch/config.yaml), project config (.orch/config.yaml), infrastructure escape hatch, daemon defaults
- Design a single precedence model (likely: explicit flags > user config > project config > defaults)
- Design a single resolution function that every spawn path calls
- Account for the infrastructure escape hatch (when/whether it should override)
- Identify which of the 3 open bugs (1047, 1048, 1049) this design would close

PRIOR ART (closed bugs in this area):
- orch-go-1011: default_model forced wrong backend
- orch-go-1020: explicit flags overridden by escape hatch
- orch-go-1028: daemon ignores default_model
- orch-go-1045: escape hatch overrides user config
- orch-go-1046: full audit investigation (read this first)
- orch-go-1051, 1055: headless spawn silent failures

OPEN BUGS this should address:
- orch-go-1047: project spawn_mode overrides user backend
- orch-go-1048: malformed userconfig silently ignored
- orch-go-1049: user default_model not treated as explicit

DELIVERABLE: Architecture decision in .kb/decisions/ with precedence model, resolution function signature, and migration path from current ad-hoc resolution.

Tests: not run (design-only). No discovered work.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
EXPLAIN-BACK:
Unified spawn config precedence model: 6 layers with explicitness metadata so defaults never override user intent. Addresses all 10 spawn config bugs. Project config ranks above user config but Dylan doesn't use project configs currently. Infra escape hatch demoted to last resort. Incremental 5-step migration.

title: Rebase OpenCode fork onto upstream/dev — prerequisite for session metadata API

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: The OpenCode fork is 384+ commits behind upstream/dev as of Feb 14. 13 custom commits need cherry-picking back. This rebase is prerequisite for all session metadata work. RISK: ORCH_WORKER server-side code was already lost in a previous rebase. Must verify session management code hasn't changed upstream before modifying. CONSTRAINT: Must be done manually with careful conflict resolution — not a daemon-spawn task.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Rebase OpenCode fork (dev branch) onto upstream/dev.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: This rebase unblocks the session metadata integration that breaks a 5-iteration cache/drift cycle in orch-go agent observability. The fork already has critical custom features (session metadata, TTL, worker detection) that must survive the rebase.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/opencode

## Current State
- Fork branch: dev (22 custom commits ahead of upstream/dev)
- Upstream: 268 commits ahead of fork
- Remotes: origin=sst/opencode, upstream=sst/opencode, fork=dylan-conlin/opencode
- Previous rebase LOST the ORCH_WORKER server-side code — be extremely careful

## The 22 Custom Commits to Preserve
b80de167a fix: exit TUI when current session is deleted remotely
b6823f82b fix: State.create clears cache on rejected Promise init
46ea18b4b probe: OpenAI/Codex plugin model resolution
e87c20f7b fix: add max retry limit to prevent infinite retry on API 500 errors
68fc63fb9 fix(tui): stop truncating last table row after streaming completes
0922edfe7 fix: complete worker detection chain - pass session metadata through plugin hooks
459a1bfba fix: read x-opencode-env-ORCH_WORKER header and set session.metadata.role
d1409443d fix: add missing time_ttl migration + CLAUDE.md schema constraint
f3c3865b8 feat(session): Add TTL with periodic cleanup and SSE events
3ea245f6f fix: rebind session_delete from ctrl+d to leader-d
36f084ca5 feat: add optional metadata field to Session.Info schema
a95e44254 fix(config): skip .test.ts/.spec.ts files from plugin directories
9e6648265 fix(server): catch unhandled AbortError from fire-and-forget prompt_async cancellation
c3c84c411 fix: add instance eviction (LRU/TTL) + SSE cleanup to prevent 8.4GB memory growth
006c9e83f fix(server): bound instance cache with LRU eviction, fix SSE teardown on dispose
98f9e89dc feat(server): log periodic memory usage for leak diagnosis
51a01820d fix(tui): clean up error rendering for DOMException and NotFoundError
f07f77aa6 fix(sdk): make SSE reconnection fix permanent in build process
d9542ceed investigation: verify OAuth priority fix was lost from dev branch
fa16b9dbe fix: forward ORCH_WORKER env var as x-opencode-env-ORCH_WORKER header
59f006640 test: add tests for session.metadata.role from x-opencode-env-ORCH_WORKER header
a82f95816 feat(stealth): implement full pi-ai stealth mode parity for Claude Max OAuth

## Procedure
1. git checkout dev
2. Create backup: git branch pre-rebase-backup-feb18
3. git fetch upstream
4. Rebase: git rebase upstream/dev
5. Resolve conflicts — PRESERVE ALL CUSTOM COMMITS. When in doubt, keep our version.
6. After rebase completes: bun install && bun run build (in packages/opencode/)
7. Verify build passes
8. DO NOT PUSH. Leave for Dylan to review and push.

## Critical Files to Watch
- packages/opencode/src/session/ — our metadata + TTL changes live here
- packages/opencode/src/server/ — our instance eviction + ORCH_WORKER header
- packages/opencode/src/plugin/ — our worker detection chain
- drizzle migrations — our time_ttl migration must survive

## Success Criteria
- All 22 custom commits present in git log after rebase
- bun run build succeeds
- No custom code silently dropped (verify session metadata, TTL, worker detection all still present)
- Backup branch exists at pre-rebase-backup-feb18

IN SCOPE: Rebase only. Resolve conflicts.
OUT OF SCOPE: New features, code changes beyond conflict resolution, pushing.

title: ADR: Two-lane agent discovery with atomic spawn, reason codes, and regression guardrails

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: This is the architectural decision that breaks the 5-iteration cache/remove cycle in agent observability. Dylan spent 6 weeks (Dec 21 - Feb 18) fighting ghost agents, phantom status, and silent enrichment failures caused by multi-source reconciliation. Five local state layers (registry.json, sessions.json, state.db, workspace cache, multi-source reconciliation) were each built to solve slow/incomplete queries and each drifted from reality.

The current beads-first solution (Feb 18) correctly inverts the query direction but has two unresolved pressure points: --no-track agents are invisible, and orchestrator sessions don't fit the beads work-item model. These are the exact gaps that triggered cache-building in January. Without addressing them, the cycle restarts.

## Deliverable

Write an ADR at .kb/decisions/2026-02-18-two-lane-agent-discovery.md with an acceptance test matrix. The ADR must cover all 6 design points below.

## Design (from Codex analysis, validated by orchestrator)

### 1. Lock domain boundaries (enforceable, not just preferred)

- beads = lifecycle for tracked work (exists/done)
- workspace manifest = binding (beads_id <-> session_id <-> project_dir)  
- OpenCode = liveness only
- tmux = presentation only
- No other persisted lifecycle state allowed

### 2. Two-lane split: tracked work vs untracked sessions

- orch status / dashboard /api/agents: tracked agents only (must have beads_id)
- orch sessions / /api/sessions: orchestrator, ad-hoc, --no-track sessions
- Stop trying to merge these into one truth view
- This resolves the Jan 5 semantic mismatch ('orchestrators are not issues') that killed beads-first the first time

### 3. Atomic spawn (all-or-nothing)

On spawn, require all 3 writes succeed or spawn fails:
  1. beads issue/tag or beads linkage
  2. workspace manifest with beads_id, session_id, project_dir
  3. spawn evidence marker
No partial state. A half-spawned agent is worse than a failed spawn.

Tradeoff to document: beads availability becomes a hard dependency. If beads is down, spawn fails. This is intentional — partial state caused the 238-dead-agents bug (orch-go-1074).

### 4. Single-pass query with reason codes

issues := beads.ListInProgress(tag='orch:agent')
bindings := workspace.LookupByBeadsIDs(issues.IDs)
live := opencode.BatchLiveness(bindings.SessionIDs)
return JoinWithReasonCodes(issues, bindings, live)

Never return silent empty metadata. Return explicit reason codes (missing_binding, missing_session, session_dead, etc.). Every previous debugging session was caused by silent enrichment failures.

### 5. Performance without drift

- Allow ONLY in-memory TTL cache (seconds), process-local, non-authoritative
- No disk-backed projection cache for lifecycle data
- Dashboard server (long-lived process) benefits from in-memory cache
- CLI commands (short-lived process) hit full query cost each invocation — acceptable
- Target: dashboard <500ms, CLI <2s (currently 4s)

### 6. Regression guardrails

- Contract tests: spawn -> visible, complete -> gone, restart resilience, cross-project --workdir, concurrent spawns
- Architecture lint rule: forbid new persistent lifecycle store packages (pkg/state/, pkg/registry/, pkg/cache/ etc)
- These are structural gates, not reminders. Agents ignore reminders under pressure.

## Acceptance Test Matrix (include in ADR)

| Scenario | Lane | Expected | Reason code if missing |
|----------|------|----------|----------------------|
| Tracked agent spawned | orch status | visible with full metadata | — |
| Tracked agent completed | orch status | gone (issue closed) | — |
| --no-track agent spawned | orch sessions | visible | — |
| --no-track agent spawned | orch status | NOT visible (by design) | — |
| Orchestrator session | orch sessions | visible | — |
| Beads down during spawn | — | spawn fails with error | — |
| OpenCode down | orch status | agents shown, status=unknown | missing_session |
| Workspace missing | orch status | agent shown, metadata partial | missing_binding |
| Cross-project --workdir | orch status | correct project_dir | — |
| Concurrent spawns (5x) | orch status | all 5 visible, no duplicates | — |
| Server restart | orch status | agents survive, no ghosts | — |
| New pkg/state/ file added | CI | lint failure, blocked | — |

## Complementary work (separate track, not in this ADR)

orch-go-1078/1079/1080: OpenCode fork session metadata API. Makes the authoritative source richer so the workspace cross-reference step eventually becomes unnecessary. The ADR should note this as a future simplification but not depend on it.

## References

- .kb/investigations/2025-12-21-synthesis-registry-evolution-and-orch-identity.md (full cycle history)
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-18-design-agent-observability-rethink.md (beads-first design)
- .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-lifecycle-ownership-own-accept-build.md (Own/Accept/Build)
- .kb/decisions/2026-01-12-registry-is-spawn-cache.md (the decision that defended the registry)
- orch-go-1074 (238 dead agents from partial state)
- orch-go-1058 (strategic question that led to beads-first)

title: Architect: atomic spawn with workspace manifest and orch:agent tagging

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: This is the foundation of the two-lane agent discovery architecture. Current spawn is fire-and-forget — partial state caused the 238-dead-agents bug (1074). This refactor makes spawn atomic: all 3 writes succeed or spawn fails entirely.

DESIGN (from ADR .kb/decisions/2026-02-18-two-lane-agent-discovery.md):

1. ATOMIC SPAWN: On spawn, require all writes succeed or rollback:
   a) Create/tag beads issue with orch:agent
   b) Write workspace manifest (beads_id, session_id, project_dir, tier, spawn_mode)
   c) Create OpenCode session with metadata (beads_id, workspace_path, tier)
   d) Write spawn evidence marker
   If any fail, rollback all previous steps. No partial state.

2. WORKSPACE MANIFEST: Structured binding file in .orch/workspace/{name}/manifest.json
   - Must support batch lookup by beads_id (workspace.LookupByBeadsIDs)
   - Contains: beads_id, session_id, project_dir, spawn_time, tier, spawn_mode

3. SESSION METADATA: At spawn, store in OpenCode session.metadata:
   beads_id, workspace_path, tier (API verified in 1079 probe)

4. orch:agent TAG: All tracked agents get this beads tag for efficient listing

ARCHITECT FIRST: spawn_cmd.go is likely a hotspot. Check line count. If >1,500 lines, extract spawn pipeline before adding atomic spawn. See .kb/guides/code-extraction-patterns.md.

REFERENCES: ADR sections 'Atomic Spawn' and 'Domain Boundaries'. Pseudocode in ADR.

title: Implement single-pass query engine with reason codes (queryTrackedAgents)

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: This is the query engine that replaces ad-hoc cross-referencing with a single deterministic path. Every previous debugging session was caused by silent enrichment failures — this function makes failure modes visible via reason codes.

DESIGN (from ADR section 'Single-Pass Query with Reason Codes'):

AgentStatus struct:
  BeadsID, SessionID, Status, Phase, ProjectDir
  MissingBinding bool  // workspace manifest not found
  MissingSession bool  // OpenCode session not found
  SessionDead bool     // session idle/errored
  MissingPhase bool    // no Phase comment in beads
  Reason string        // human-readable

queryTrackedAgents(projectDirs []string) ([]AgentStatus, error):
  1. beads.ListByTag('orch:agent', status='in_progress')
  2. workspace.LookupByBeadsIDs(issueIDs)
  3. opencode.BatchLiveness(sessionIDs)
  4. JoinWithReasonCodes(issues, bindings, liveness)

DEGRADED MODES:
  - OpenCode down → agents shown with status=unknown
  - Workspace missing → agent shown with reason=missing_binding
  - Never return silent empty metadata

DEPENDS ON: 1083 (atomic spawn creates the workspace manifests this queries)
REFERENCE: ADR pseudocode for queryTrackedAgents

The atomic spawn infrastructure is now in place (1083 completed): AgentManifest has SessionID, LookupManifestsByBeadsIDs exists in pkg/spawn/session.go, beads orch:agent tagging works. Build queryTrackedAgents: 1) beads.ListByTag('orch:agent', status=in_progress) 2) LookupManifestsByBeadsIDs 3) opencode.BatchLiveness 4) JoinWithReasonCodes. AgentStatus struct needs: BeadsID, SessionID, Status, Phase, ProjectDir, MissingBinding, MissingSession, SessionDead, MissingPhase, Reason. Degraded modes: OpenCode down → status=unknown, workspace missing → reason=missing_binding. Never return silent empty metadata.

title: Gate: contract tests for 12-scenario acceptance matrix + architecture lint rule

comments.author: dylanconlin

FRAME: Structural gates that prevent the 6th iteration of the cache cycle. Without these, the next agent under pressure will add 'just a small cache' and restart the drift.

SCOPE:

   - Tracked agent: spawn→visible, complete→gone
   - --no-track: visible in orch sessions, NOT in orch status

   - Cross-project --workdir: correct project_dir
   - Concurrent spawns: no duplicates
   - Server restart: no ghosts

2. Architecture lint rule in CI:
   Block PRs adding pkg/state/, pkg/registry/, pkg/cache/, or persistent .json/.db files in ~/.orch/ for lifecycle state.

DEPENDS ON: 1086, 1087 (both lanes implemented)
TYPE: gate — orchestrator reviews before closing
REFERENCE: ADR sections 'Contract Tests' and 'Architecture Lint Rule'

title: Work-graph UI: replace hierarchy connectors with flow connectors

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
DESIGN DIRECTION: Separate grouping from flow in dependency visualization.

Problem: Tree connectors (├── └──) imply hierarchy but dependencies have direction. The UI conflates two jobs: grouping (what belongs together) and flow (what order things happen in).

What's invisible today:
1. Direction — does the gate wait for children or vice versa?
2. Inter-sibling flow — do siblings depend on each other?
3. Critical path — which chain determines when the gate closes?

Solution: Keep vertical list layout, fix connectors and ordering.
- Topologically sort children (upstream first, downstream last)
- Replace hierarchy connectors with flow connectors (top-to-bottom = dependency direction)
- Parallel items sit at same level or get fork indicator
- Gate sits below a separator ('everything above must complete before this closes')

No horizontal overflow. Same page layout. Changes are sort order + connector glyphs.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Redesign work-graph dependency visualization: replace hierarchy connectors with flow connectors.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: The work-graph UI currently uses tree connectors to show dependencies, but trees imply hierarchy while dependencies have direction. Users can't tell which way blocking flows, can't see inter-sibling dependencies, and can't identify the critical path.

2. Replace hierarchy connectors with flow connectors — top-to-bottom = dependency flow direction
3. Parallel items (no dependency between them) sit at same visual level or get a fork indicator
4. Gate issues sit below a separator line — visual signal that everything above must complete before this closes

IN SCOPE:
- Work-graph component rendering logic
- Connector glyphs and sort order within dependency groups
- Gate separator visual

OUT SCOPE:
- Changing the overall page layout (stays vertical list)

title: Architect: extract daemon config into cohesive package

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Adding a single daemon config boolean (reflect_open) required touching 10+ files and caused a 526K token agent spiral (ek0b). The daemon config surface is too distributed — struct, defaults, CLI flags, plist template, plist parsing, doctor.go, serve_system.go API, userconfig.go, config_cmd.go show, plus tests for each. This is the same accretion gravity pattern that hit spawn_cmd.go.

ARCHITECT SCOPE:
1. Map the current config flow: where does a new daemon flag need to be added today?
2. Identify extraction targets: can config definition, serialization, and display be consolidated?
3. Design a cohesive daemon config package where adding a flag is 1-2 file touches, not 10+
4. Consider: single source of truth for flag definitions that generates CLI flags, plist entries, API fields, and show output

title: E2E test script for two-lane agent discovery

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: The two-lane discovery chain passed all unit/contract tests but the live probe found integration bugs. We need a durable E2E test script that exercises the real system end-to-end.

SCOPE: Shell script (tests/e2e_two_lane.sh or similar) that:
1. Creates a beads issue + spawns tracked agent → polls orch status until visible → verifies metadata + reason codes
2. Completes the agent → verifies it disappears from orch status
3. Spawns --no-track → verifies it shows in orch sessions but NOT orch status
4. Tests degraded modes: kill OpenCode → verify status=unknown (not silent empty)
5. Tests dashboard APIs: curl /api/agents and /api/sessions, verify responses match CLI
6. Cleanup: close test issues, clean test workspaces

REQUIRES: Running OpenCode server, beads, dashboard. NOT a unit test — a real integration script.
DEPENDS ON: 1093 and 1094 should be fixed first (bugs in API layer), or the E2E script should document expected failures.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Write E2E test script for two-lane agent discovery. Issue orch-go-1095.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: The two-lane chain passed unit/contract tests but a live probe caught two integration bugs (12h time filter, closed-issue filter). We need a durable E2E script that exercises the real system so these bugs get caught mechanically, not by manual probing.

DELIVERABLE: tests/e2e_two_lane.sh (executable shell script)

SCENARIOS TO TEST:
1. Lane 1 — tracked agent lifecycle:
   a. bd create test issue + orch spawn --issue → poll orch status until visible → verify metadata fields present
   b. orch complete the test agent → verify it disappears from orch status
   c. Verify it does NOT appear in orch sessions (two-lane separation)

2. Lane 2 — untracked sessions:
   a. orch sessions returns results → verify session count > 0
   b. Verify untracked sessions do NOT appear in orch status (two-lane separation)

3. Dashboard API parity:
   a. curl -sk https://localhost:3348/api/agents → verify JSON, non-empty for active agents
   b. curl -sk https://localhost:3348/api/sessions → verify JSON, matches orch sessions count

4. Degraded mode (if feasible):
   a. With no active agents → orch status returns clean empty (no ghosts, no errors)

CLEANUP: Script must clean up any test issues/workspaces it creates (bd close, workspace removal).

REQUIREMENTS:
- Script must be runnable with: bash tests/e2e_two_lane.sh
- Uses exit codes (0 = pass, 1 = fail)
- Prints clear PASS/FAIL per scenario
- Requires running infrastructure (OpenCode, beads, dashboard) — document this in script header

EXISTING PATTERNS: See tests/ directory for any existing test scripts.

description: Phase 3 of lifecycle ownership boundaries decision. comments.text:
FRAME: Investigate why daemon-spawned agents stall on opencode/GPT-5.2 backend (orch-go-1104).

ORIENTATION_FRAME: The daemon's primary spawn path is broken — 3/3 agents spawned via opencode backend with GPT-5.2 stalled in Planning phase within ~1 minute, consumed 41-145K tokens, then went idle with no recovery. Need root cause before building resilience.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Small

FORENSIC EVIDENCE (read these first — these are session transcripts from the stalled agents):
1. .orch/workspace/og-feat-getallsessionstatus-fetches-sessions-19feb-72fd/SESSION_LOG.md (1099 - 41K tokens)
2. .orch/workspace/og-feat-workspace-lookup-historical-19feb-2166/SESSION_LOG.md (1098 - 43K tokens)
3. .orch/workspace/og-feat-architect-extract-daemon-19feb-2b34/SESSION_LOG.md (1092 - 145K tokens)

All 3 were spawned by daemon via opencode backend, defaulted to GPT-5.2 model. All stalled in Planning phase.

QUESTIONS TO ANSWER:
1. What did the agents actually do before stalling? (Read the session logs)
2. Did they hit an error? Rate limit? Context window limit?
3. Is the spawn context (81KB SPAWN_CONTEXT.md) too large for GPT-5.2?
4. Is there a pattern in HOW they stalled (same error, same point in conversation)?
5. Is this a GPT-5.2 specific issue or an opencode backend issue?

Also check:
- How does the daemon spawn path differ from manual orch spawn? (look at daemon.go and pkg/daemon/)
- Does the opencode backend handle errors/timeouts differently than claude backend?

DELIVERABLES:
- Root cause analysis with evidence from the session logs
- Recommendation for fix (daemon health check? backend fallback? context size limit?)
- .kb/investigations/ artifact

title: Fix config precedence: runWork() elevates default_model to CLI priority, bypassing project config opencode.model
description: In cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go:429-436, runWork() loads user config default_model into spawnModel package-level var, which enters resolve pipeline as CLI.Model. This gives it highest priority, silently overriding project config opencode.model. Fix: let default_model flow through normal resolve pipeline.
repro: Set default_model: codex in ~/.orch/config.yaml and opencode.model: flash in .orch/config.yaml. Run orch work on any issue. Observe model resolves to codex (CLI priority) instead of flash (project config).

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Fix config precedence: runWork() elevates default_model to CLI priority, bypassing project config opencode.model

In cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go:429-436, runWork() loads user config default_model into spawnModel package-level var, which enters resolve pipeline as CLI.Model. This gives it highest priority, silently overriding project config opencode.model. Fix: let default_model flow through normal resolve pipeline.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Fix config precedence bug: runWork() elevates default_model to CLI priority (orch-go-1105).

ORIENTATION_FRAME: Daemon agents get the wrong model because runWork() treats user config default_model as CLI-level priority, silently overriding project config opencode.model. This caused 3 dead agents on GPT-5.2 when project config intended flash.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Small

THE BUG (exact location):
cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go:429-436 — runWork() loads user config default_model into the spawnModel package-level var. This enters the resolve pipeline as CLI.Model which has highest priority, overriding project config opencode.model.

THE FIX:
runWork() should NOT set spawnModel from default_model. Instead, let default_model flow through the normal resolve pipeline where project config opencode.model can override it. The resolve precedence should be: CLI flag > project config opencode.model > user config default_model > hardcoded default.

REPRODUCTION:
1. Set default_model: codex in ~/.orch/config.yaml
2. Set opencode.model: flash in .orch/config.yaml
3. Run orch daemon preview or orch work on any issue
4. Observe model resolves to codex instead of flash

VERIFICATION:
1. After fix: orch daemon preview should show flash (from project config), not codex
2. orch spawn --model opus should still override everything (CLI flag highest priority)
3. If no project config opencode.model, default_model should still apply as fallback

DELIVERABLES:
- Fix in spawn_cmd.go
- Test covering resolve precedence
- .orch/workspace/fix-config-precedence/SYNTHESIS.md

title: Design coupling hotspot analysis (co-change clusters + agent spiral mining)

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Agents spiral when they encounter cross-surface coupling (e.g. daemon config: 12 files for 1 boolean, 526K tokens). Size hotspots don't catch this. We need a coupling-aware analysis that combines git co-change clusters with agent spiral evidence to predict where agents will struggle before they spiral.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Design a coupling hotspot analysis system for orch-go.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: Agents spiral when they hit cross-surface coupling — the daemon config problem (12 files for 1 boolean, 526K token spiral) is invisible to our size-based hotspot check. We need tooling that predicts where agents will struggle before they spiral.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Medium

## Context

We have an existing 'orch hotspot' command that measures file SIZE (>1500 lines = extraction needed). This catches accretion but misses coupling — where a single concept spans many files across layers (CLI cmd/, API serve_*, pkg/, web/, tests).

The daemon config investigation (.kb/investigations/2026-02-19-design-extract-daemon-config-package.md) is the canonical example: adding 1 boolean requires touching 10-12 files, none individually large. Agent ek0b spiraled at 526K tokens trying to discover the full touch surface.

## Design Questions

1. **Co-change cluster detection**: How should we mine git history to find files that always change together? What's the right granularity (file-level? function-level? struct-level?)? What threshold makes a cluster actionable vs noise?

2. **Agent spiral evidence**: We have events.jsonl (lifecycle events) and workspace ACTIVITY.json files. How do we extract 'spiral' signal — high token count, long duration, abandoned agents — and correlate it with files touched?

3. **Integration with existing hotspot**: Should this be a new command (orch coupling-hotspots) or extend orch hotspot with a --coupling flag? How does the output look — ranked list of concept clusters with coupling score?

4. **Actionability**: When a coupling cluster is detected, what's the recommended action? Not all coupling is bad — test files co-change with source files. What distinguishes 'healthy coupling' from 'agent-hostile coupling'?

5. **Cross-surface concept mapping**: The interesting clusters span pkg/, cmd/, web/. How do we name/identify the 'concept' a cluster represents (e.g., 'daemon config', 'agent status', 'spawn config')?

## Scope

IN: Design the analysis approach, output format, integration point, actionability criteria
OUT: Implementation (separate issue), modifying existing hotspot code, building the frontend

## Deliverables

- SYNTHESIS.md in workspace with:
  - Recommended approach for co-change analysis
  - Agent spiral mining design
  - Output format and integration with orch hotspot
  - Criteria for distinguishing healthy vs hostile coupling
  - Estimated implementation scope

## Constraints

- Read .kb/investigations/2026-02-19-design-extract-daemon-config-package.md for the canonical coupling example
- Read .kb/decisions/2026-02-18-two-lane-agent-discovery.md for the other major debt area
- Check existing orch hotspot implementation for integration point
- Check events.jsonl and ACTIVITY.json formats for available signal

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Planning - Reading canonical coupling example, existing hotspot impl, and event formats

comments.author: dylanconlin

Show the actual call chains with file paths and line numbers. Identify any gaps in the wiring - places where account resolution should happen but doesn't. Is the heuristic dead code, partially integrated, or fully wired?

Focus on: completeness of the integration, not implementation details. Show where it's hooked up (or not).

title: Dashboard hides idle/at-risk agents — stuck agents invisible
description: Dashboard at localhost:5188 doesn't show agents with idle status. orch status --all shows 3 idle agents (pw-8962 AT-RISK, pw-8961 CRITICAL at 290K tokens, pw-d18e AT-RISK) but dashboard shows none. The agents most needing attention (stuck, spiraling) are the ones the dashboard hides. Dashboard should surface idle/at-risk agents prominently, not filter them out.
repro: 1. Spawn agents that go idle (e.g. stuck in Planning phase). 2. Run orch status --all — agents visible with AT-RISK/CRITICAL flags. 3. Open dashboard at localhost:5188 — no agents shown.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Dashboard hides idle/at-risk agents (orch-go-1112). orch status --all shows agents with idle status and AT-RISK/CRITICAL risk flags, but the dashboard at localhost:5188 shows no agents. The dashboard gets data from orch serve (port 3348). Investigate: 1) What API endpoint does the dashboard call for agent data? 2) Does the API filter out idle agents? 3) Does the frontend filter them? The fix: idle/at-risk agents should be PROMINENTLY shown in the dashboard, not hidden. Repro: spawn agents that go idle, confirm orch status --all sees them, confirm dashboard doesn't. Verification: idle agents appear in dashboard after fix.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Planning - Investigating dashboard agent filtering. Will trace API endpoint, backend filtering, and frontend rendering to find where idle/at-risk agents are hidden.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Implementing - Root cause identified: (1) Time filter (?since=12h) excludes dead agents whose updated_at is older than 12h. (2) No context risk assessment in dashboard API, so AT-RISK/CRITICAL flags never shown. (3) Dead agents need attention but get hidden by progressive disclosure defaults. Fix: bypass time filter for attention-needing agents + add context risk to API + frontend display.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Complete - Fixed 3 compounding issues hiding idle/at-risk agents from dashboard: (1) time filter bypass for dead/awaiting-cleanup/at-risk agents, (2) context risk computation in API, (3) frontend at-risk section + risk badges. Build passes, frontend compiles clean.

Dashboard now surfaces idle/at-risk agents. Three root causes fixed: idle→dead mapping, time filter bypass, context risk in API.
